questions.question: How did Romulus die?
questions.ideal_answer: The sources disagree. The official version, in both Livy and Plutarch, is that while Romulus was reviewing the army at the Goat's Marsh a sudden storm enveloped him and he was carried up to heaven, thereafter worshipped as the god Quirinus. But both historians also preserve a darker rumor: that the senators (fathers), resentful of his rule, fell upon him and tore his body to pieces, each carrying away a part — Plutarch placing the deed in the temple of Vulcan.

questions.question: How did Themistocles die?
questions.ideal_answer: Thucydides states plainly that disease was the real cause of his death, noting only as a rumor the story that he poisoned himself on finding he could not deliver Greece to the Persian king. Plutarch gives the dramatic suicide as "the usual story" — that Themistocles sacrificed, feasted with his friends, and drank bull's blood — while acknowledging the alternative that it was a fast-acting poison.

questions.question: Did Caligula actually intend to make his horse consul?
questions.ideal_answer: The two sources differ in confidence. Suetonius reports it only as hearsay — "it is even said that he intended to make him consul." Cassius Dio states it more strongly: that Caligula promised the consulship to the horse Incitatus and "would certainly have done" it had he lived longer. So the claim is a guarded rumor in Suetonius and a near-assertion in Dio.

questions.question: How did Bucephalus die?
questions.ideal_answer: The sources disagree on whether the horse died of battle wounds or of old age. Arrian insists explicitly that Bucephalas died "not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age." Plutarch reports both: that most authorities say he died under treatment for his wounds after the battle with Porus, though Onesicritus says it was fatigue and age. Both agree he was about thirty and that Alexander founded a city (Bucephala) in his memory.

questions.question: How did Xerxes get back to Asia after Salamis?
questions.ideal_answer: Herodotus himself records two conflicting accounts. In his main narrative, Xerxes marched his army by land to the Hellespont and crossed to Abydos by ship, the bridges having been broken by a storm. But he then reports "another story": that Xerxes sailed from Eion on the Strymon in a Phoenician ship caught in a storm, and that the Persian nobles on deck leapt into the sea to lighten it and save the king — a tale Herodotus relates but disbelieves.

Smith's history follows the leaping-the-wall version as the standard one.

questions.question: How did Alexander deal with the Gordian knot?
questions.ideal_answer: The sources split on whether he cut it or untied it by a trick. Arrian reports that some say Alexander, unable to loosen the cord, struck it with his sword and cut it through — but that Aristobulus says he pulled out the pin of the wagon-pole and so drew out the yoke. Plutarch gives the same two versions: most authors say he cut it with his sword, but Aristobulus says he undid it easily by pulling out the pin.

questions.question: What did Nero say about an artist dying, and when?
questions.ideal_answer: The wording and timing differ between sources. Suetonius reports that as his grave was dug before his eyes Nero wept and kept repeating "What an artist is now about to perish!" Cassius Dio gives a different form and moment: that Nero spoke the "far-famed sentence" — "Jupiter, what an artist perishes in me!" — at the very instant he killed himself.

questions.question: Why did Tarpeia betray the citadel, and how did she die?
questions.ideal_answer: All sources agree she was bribed by the gold the Sabines wore on their left arms and was crushed to death under their shields, but they differ on her intent. Livy gives the standard treacherous version (she was bribed to admit the soldiers and crushed under their arms) but also reports an alternative: that she demanded their shields in good faith, meaning to disarm them, and was killed as if a traitor by the very reward she chose. Plutarch agrees she coveted the golden bracelets and asked for "the things they wore on their left arms."

questions.question: What was Lycurgus' date and life story?
questions.ideal_answer: The sources cannot agree on almost anything about him. Plutarch opens his Life by warning that scarcely anything asserted by one historian is not contradicted by another — as to his family, his travels, the manner of his death, and above all the very age in which he lived. Grote sharpens the specific disagreement: Herodotus makes Lycurgus uncle and guardian of king Labotas of the Agid line (placing him about 996 BC), whereas all other accounts make him a younger brother of the rival Proclid line.

questions.question: Who was to blame for losing the battle of Cannae?
questions.ideal_answer: Both sources fault the consul Varro (Gaius Terentius), but with different emphasis. Polybius lays the disaster squarely on him personally — his flight to Venusia was "as disgraceful to himself as his conduct in office had been disastrous to his country." Mommsen, while equally scornful of "the hero of the pavement," frames the deeper fault as systemic: the old custom of alternating supreme command between the two consuls day by day, which forced the cautious Paullus to yield to Varro's will.

questions.question: How do the sources judge Alcibiades?
questions.ideal_answer: Thucydides judges that his lawless personal extravagance — horses and expenditure beyond his means — made the people fear him as an aspiring tyrant, "and this later on had not a little to do with the ruin of the Athenian state," though his public war conduct was excellent. Plutarch fixes on his chameleon-like versatility, adapting to Spartan austerity or Persian pomp at will. Grote, the modern voice, grants him boldness, resource, and vigor "not surpassed by any contemporary Greek" while damning his want of public principle.

questions.question: How do the sources judge Themistocles?
questions.ideal_answer: Herodotus reports he was esteemed throughout Hellas as "the ablest by far of the Hellenes" and uniquely honored at Sparta. Thucydides' obituary calls his untaught native genius for instant judgment and prediction "quite extraordinary and unparalleled." Grote balances the picture: his rapacity made him hated and feared among the allies, and the suspicions of treasonable medism at Athens grew from his own corrupt and double-faced conduct.

questions.question: How is the disaster at Cannae described?
questions.ideal_answer: Polybius gives the soldier's accounting: of six thousand Roman horse only seventy escaped with Varro, the infantry "died nobly" to the number of seventy thousand, both sides fighting with conspicuous gallantry. Dio's fragment calls it the most fearful disaster Rome ever suffered, preceded by portents and Sibylline prophecy. Mommsen, the modern judge, treats the "unexampled success" as the moment Hannibal's grand political combination against Rome seemed to mature.

questions.question: How do historians ancient and modern assess Pericles?
questions.ideal_answer: Thucydides' famous verdict: by rank, ability, and known integrity he led the multitude instead of being led, so that "what was nominally a democracy became in his hands government by the first citizen." Plutarch gives the deathbed self-assessment — his proudest claim was that "no Athenian, through my means, ever wore mourning." Grote notes that his immense ascendancy and unparalleled eloquence are attested even more forcibly by his enemies than his friends.

questions.question: What did ancient writers know and say about Britain and its people?
questions.ideal_answer: Caesar, the first eyewitness, reports inland Britons who sow no corn, live on milk and flesh, dress in skins, dye themselves blue with woad, and hold wives in common. Tacitus weighs their origins from physical type — the ruddy-haired Caledonians suggesting German stock, the swarthy Silures Iberian. Strabo maps the island as a triangle parallel to the Gallic coast, roughly 4,300–4,400 stadia along its longest side.

questions.question: How is Cleopatra's death reported across the sources?
questions.ideal_answer: All three sources report the famous asp while admitting uncertainty. Plutarch describes her found dead in royal ornaments on a golden bed, Iras dying at her feet and Charmion adjusting her diadem — but says "what really took place is known to no one," weighing the asp in the figs against poison in a hollow bodkin. Dio likewise: "no one knows clearly in what manner she perished," only slight indentations on her arm. Suetonius adds that Augustus, wanting her alive for his triumph, sent the Psylli snake-charmers to suck out the poison.

questions.question: What do the sources say about Alexander's final illness and death?
questions.ideal_answer: Both Plutarch and Arrian follow the Royal Diary: a fever after drinking with Medius, worsening day by day as he continued bathing and sacrificing, until he lay speechless — Arrian preserving the scene of the soldiers filing past while he could only greet them with his eyes — and died. Both record the later poisoning story; Plutarch calls it "a mere made-up story," noting the body stayed uncorrupted for days, and Arrian notes Aristobulus and Ptolemy agree with the diary, adding only the alleged last word that the kingdom would go "to the best."

questions.question: How do Roman writers describe the Germans' way of fighting?
questions.ideal_answer: Caesar, from battlefield experience, describes them rapidly forming a phalanx so dense his soldiers leapt onto it to tear away shields from above. Tacitus, the ethnographer, details the framea — the short, sharp, all-purpose spear used at close quarters or thrown — the wedge-shaped battle line, the doctrine that giving ground to rally again is prudence rather than cowardice, and the carrying off of their dead mid-battle.

questions.question: How was Caesar's funeral described, and what did it unleash?
questions.ideal_answer: Suetonius describes the planned pyre in the Campus Martius overtaken by the crowd: two armed men fired the bier before the Rostra and the throng heaped on benches and court furniture; afterwards the populace ran with torches to the houses of Brutus and Cassius and lynched the wrong Cinna. Plutarch adds the cause: Antony's funeral oration, climaxing in the unfolding of Caesar's bloody, pierced garment, after which the mob burned the body on an improvised pyre and ran with the half-burnt brands to fire the murderers' houses.

questions.question: What did ancient writers believe about the sources and flooding of the Nile?
questions.ideal_answer: Herodotus reviews three Greek theories of the summer flood — the Etesian winds, the world-encircling Ocean, and melting snow — refutes them all (offering his own sun-driven explanation), and reports that no Egyptian, Libyan, or Greek professed to know the river's sources except a Sais scribe he didn't believe. Strabo, writing centuries later, says what the ancients only conjectured had become eyewitness knowledge: the Nile owes its rise to summer rains falling in the mountains of Upper Ethiopia.

questions.question: Whom did the Athenians send as a runner to Sparta before the battle of Marathon, and what happened to him on the way?
questions.ideal_answer: The Athenian generals sent Pheidippides, a professional long-distance runner, as herald to Sparta. On the way, by Mount Parthenion above Tegea, the god Pan met him, called him by name, and bade him ask the Athenians why they paid him no honor although he was well disposed to them and had often been — and would again be — serviceable.

questions.question: How did the statesman who championed Athens' Long Walls die?
questions.ideal_answer: Pericles — who, as Socrates attested, proposed the long wall to the people — died of the plague of Athens, in his case not with violent fits but as a dull, lingering distemper that slowly wasted his body and mind.

spans.quote_preview: the long wall, which Socrates says he himself heard Pericles propose to the people, was undertaken by Callicrates.

questions.question: What were the symptoms of the epidemic that devastated Athens?
questions.ideal_answer: Thucydides (who had the disease himself) describes the "distemper" thus: people in good health were suddenly attacked by violent heats in the head, redness and inflammation of the eyes, a bloody throat and tongue with fetid breath, then sneezing, hoarseness, chest pain and a hard cough, stomach upset and retching with violent spasms. Most died around the seventh to ninth day; in survivors the disease could descend into the bowels (fatal diarrhoea), or cost them fingers, toes, eyes, or memory.

questions.question: What role did mass kidnapping play in early Rome's search for wives?
questions.ideal_answer: Rome under Romulus was short of women and neighboring peoples refused intermarriage, so Romulus staged games (the Consualia) and invited the neighbors; at a signal the Roman youths rushed in and carried off the unmarried women — the "rape of the Sabine women." The ensuing war with the Sabines ended with the women themselves brokering peace and the two peoples merging.

questions.question: Does Thucydides describe anything like a tsunami?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes. He reports that during the earthquakes of 426 BC the sea at Orobiae in Euboea retired from the coast and returned in a huge wave, flooding part of the town and leaving some of it permanently under water, killing those who could not reach high ground (with a similar inundation at Atalanta). He even gives the correct causal explanation: the earthquake drives the sea back, and it recoils with redoubled force.

questions.question: How were the pontoon bridges across the Hellespont built?
questions.ideal_answer: After a storm destroyed the first pair (built by Phoenicians with flax cables and Egyptians with papyrus cables), the rebuilt bridges rested on rows of anchored fifty-oared galleys and triremes — 360 under one bridge and 314 under the other — over which cables of white flax and papyrus were stretched tight with wooden windlasses; planks, brushwood, and stamped earth were laid on top, with barriers so the animals would not see the sea.

questions.question: What solar eclipse marked the departure of Xerxes' army, and how was it interpreted?
questions.ideal_answer: As the army set out from Sardis in spring, the sun left its place in a perfectly clear sky and day became night. Xerxes, concerned, asked the Magians, who declared the god was foreshowing to the Greeks the abandonment of their cities — since the sun was the foreshower of events for the Greeks, the moon for the Persians. Xerxes marched on greatly pleased.

questions.question: How did the Persian royal postal relay system work?
questions.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, men and horses were stationed along the route, one for each day's journey; the message was carried by the first rider to the second, the second to the third, and so on, like the Greek torch-race. Neither snow, rain, heat, nor darkness of night stopped them — nothing mortal travelled faster. The Persians called it the angareion.

questions.question: How does Plutarch portray Antony's ruin through Cleopatra?
questions.ideal_answer: Plutarch frames Cleopatra as "the last and crowning mischief" that befell Antony — a love that awakened his dormant passions and corrupted what goodness and judgment remained. She kept him captive through ceaseless charm (dicing, drinking, hunting with him, never letting him escape by day or night); the passion rekindled "like Plato's restive horse" when he returned east and showered her with provinces; and after Actium it left him a misanthrope playing Timon of Athens in a sea-hut, before the final catastrophe in Alexandria.

questions.question: How does Arrian portray Alexander's bond with his soldiers?
questions.ideal_answer: As intense mutual devotion that survived even open conflict. At the Hyphasis the army's silent, grieved resistance forced the invincible king to turn back; when he nearly died among the Malli, the troops wept, cheered, and crowded to touch his hands, knees, and clothes as he showed himself alive; and at Opis the mutiny dissolved when he called every Macedonian his kinsman, sealing the reconciliation with kisses and a vast common banquet.

questions.question: How does Livy portray the struggle between patricians and plebeians?
questions.ideal_answer: As the defining, recurring affliction of the early Republic — "the discord among the several orders... the curse of this city." Driven by debt and oppression, the commons twice seceded (the first ended by Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly and the members, the second on the Sacred Mount against the decemvirs); each crisis extracted new concessions — tribunes, appeal, laws — while Livy moralizes on how hard moderation is when each side's pursuit of power or liberty makes it an object of dread to the other.

questions.question: How did the Romans become a naval power during the First Punic War, according to Polybius?
questions.ideal_answer: Through what Polybius calls extraordinary audacity. Having never thought of naval war and lacking even the knowledge to build quinqueremes, they used a wrecked Carthaginian ship as their model, trained rowers on benches set up on dry land, and invented the "crow" boarding-bridge that turned sea battles into land fights (first proven at Mylae); after catastrophic storm losses they persisted, rebuilt with lighter ships and trained crews, and won the decisive battle of Aegusa.

questions.question: How does Thucydides describe the moral collapse caused by civil war?
questions.ideal_answer: In his analysis of the Corcyra revolution, Thucydides argues that war is "a rough master" that levels men's characters to their fortunes. Words lost their ordinary meanings (recklessness became courage, moderation cowardice); religion was honored by neither side while fair phrases served guilty ends; the moderates perished between the factions; the "ancient simplicity" of honor was laughed out of existence and no man trusted his fellow. The concrete horror at Corcyra — fathers killing sons, suppliants slain at altars — set the pattern the whole Hellenic world then exceeded.

questions.question: How does Thucydides contrast the Athenian and Spartan national characters?
questions.ideal_answer: Largely through speeches. The Corinthians tell Sparta that the Athenians are addicted to innovation, swift in conception and execution, adventurous beyond their power, never at home — "born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" — while the Spartans keep what they have, invent nothing, and never go far enough. Archidamus answers by defending Spartan slowness as disciplined wisdom: warlike from self-control and honor, wise because educated too little to despise the laws.

questions.question: What does Herodotus find most remarkable about Babylon?
questions.ideal_answer: Its unmatched magnificence and its customs. He marvels at the city's scale — the deep moat and walls fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred high, and the temple of Zeus Belos with its eight stacked towers — and singles out two customs: the annual bride auction, where money from the beautiful maidens dowered the plain ones (in his opinion their wisest), and the carrying of the sick into the marketplace, since they use no physicians and every passer-by must offer advice.

questions.question: How does Suetonius chart Caligula's descent from popular prince to monster?
questions.ideal_answer: Suetonius structures the Life around an explicit pivot: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man." His accession brought genuine joy — the soldiers' beloved camp-child, heir to Germanicus' virtues — though Tiberius had already predicted he was "rearing a hydra for the people of Rome." After the pivot comes the monster: claiming divine majesty, beheading the gods' statues to mount his own head, the temple to his own divinity, and cruelties without limit.

questions.question: How does Tacitus characterize German marriage and the position of women?
questions.ideal_answer: With pointed admiration. The marriage gifts — oxen, a bridled horse, shield, spear, and sword — admonish the wife that she comes as a partner in toils and dangers, "to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war... thus she is to live; thus to die." The Germans live "fenced around with chastity": adultery is extremely rare and instantly punished, no one calls seduction the way of the world, and in the strictest tribes a woman takes one husband "as one body and one life."

questions.question: How does Suetonius portray the character of Tiberius?
questions.ideal_answer: As a dissimulating tyrant whose vices, long imperfectly concealed, flowered once he withdrew to Capri. Even Augustus reportedly pitied the Roman people for being "ground by the jaws of such a slow devourer." The soldiers' nickname Biberius marked his drunkenness from youth; at Capri, "remote from the observation of the people of Rome, he abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed"; and the translator's verdict is of an "execrable tyrant" whose indiscriminate cruelty spared neither age nor sex.
